segments.s690-g113: The prepositional phrase 'به راهب' (to the monk) marks the indirect object. 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech. 'گفت' is the simple past 'said' (verb) and the subject 'شهریار' (the king) follows the verb for poetic/literary word order; 'پس' here is temporal/connecting 'then'.
segments.s690-g114: 'که' introduces reported content ('that' / introducing what was said). 'شاداب دل' is a compact phrase where the adjective 'شاداب' (cheerful/vigorous) modifies the noun 'دل' (heart), and 'باش' is the 2nd-person imperative 'be!'. The conjunction 'و' links to the following phrase 'به روزگار', where 'به' is a preposition meaning 'with/regarding' and 'روزگار' means 'the times/one's fortunes' — an adverbial phrase indicating relation to life/fate.
segments.s690-g115: 'وزان' = 'and from that' links back to preceding place/time. 'دیر' here means 'monastery' (classical/poetic use). 'چون' is a comparative/simile 'like/as'. 'برق' is 'lightning/flash' and 'رخشان' is an adjective 'radiant/bright' modifying برق. 'زمیغ' is a noun 'uproar/din', so the sequence forms a simile describing the sound/appearance.
segments.s690-g116: 'بیامد' is the simple past 'came'. 'سوی' is the preposition 'toward(s)'. 'شارستان' is the destination 'city/town'. The conjunction 'و' coordinates the final noun 'ریغ', an archaic word here meaning rancor/malice; syntactically 'و ریغ' is coordinated with 'شارستان' (a picturesque construction listing what came toward the city).
segments.s690-g117: 'پذیره' (reception/acceptance) here functions as the idea 'welcoming/received'. 'شدندش' is past plural 'they became/they took' with the enclitic pronoun '‑ش' = 'him' attached, so together the phrase means 'they received him'. 'بزرگان شهر' is the plural subject 'the great ones / elders of the city'. Word order places the verbal phrase before or adjacent to the subject in this poetic register.
segments.s690-g118: 'کسی را' marks 'someone' as the (direct) object with the object marker 'را'. 'که' introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. 'از مردمی بود' means 'was from the people' (i.e., came from among the common people), with 'بود' as past copula. 'بهر' is an archaic preposition 'for / toward' and here leaves the clause trailing (the line continues); syntactically the relative clause qualifies 'کسی'.
segments.s690-g49: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before 'قیصر' (Caesar/emperor). 'بیابی' is the 2nd/3rd‑person present/subjunctive narrative form meaning 'you obtain'/'you may obtain' (classical/poetic usage rather than modern indicative), and it governs the following objects 'سلیح و سپاه' (weapons and troops). Word order: preposition + noun + verb + objects, typical of Ferdowsi's elevated diction.
segments.s690-g50: 'یکی' functions as 'a certain' (introducing an indefinite individual). 'دختری' is 'a maiden/daughter' (indefinite object). 'از' = 'from', and 'در' here names the court/door of the ruler (poetic use 'درِ' = palace/court). 'تاج و گاه' is a poetic binomial ('crown and court' / 'crown and place of rule'); the phrase is a locative specification ('from the court of crown and rule'). Adjective/possessive relations are compacted: 'یکی دختری از درِ تاج و گاه' = 'a certain maiden from the royal court.'
segments.s690-g51: 'چو' here means 'when'. 'با بندگان' ('with/among the servants') locates the event socially. 'کار زارت' is a nominal phrase: 'کار' = 'affair/matter' and 'زارت' = 'lamentation/wailing' — together 'the affair was one of lament.' 'بود' is the past copula 'was.' Overall: a temporal clause 'When among the servants the affair was lamentation.'
segments.s690-g52: This is a compact nominal clause: 'جهاندار' (the world‑ruler/sovereign) + 'بیدار' (awake/alert) form the subject phrase, and 'یارت بود' is the predicate: 'was your companion/ally.' 'یارت' is the possessive/relational form 'your ally,' and 'بود' is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s690-g53: 'سرانجام' = 'finally'/'in the end', functioning as a sentential adverb. 'بگریزد' is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive narrative form of 'گریختن' meaning 'will flee'/'shall flee' in this heroic-poetic register. 'آن بد نژاد' is a determiner + adjective + noun ('that ill‑born one / that bad‑bred person'). The phrase gives a future/outcome statement about that person.
segments.s690-g54: 'فراوان' is an adverb/adverbial 'often'/'plentifully'. 'کند' is the light verb 'to make/do' used with the following nouns to mean 'cause/perform' (here 'he will cause/perform frequently'). 'روز نیکیش' is 'his good day(s)' with the possessive suffix '-ش' ('نیکی' = good/auspicious + '-ش' = his). 'یاد' is 'remembrance/remembering' — together 'فراوان کند روز نیکیش یاد' = 'he will frequently remember (or cause to be remembered) his good day(s)'. Word order puts the adverb and verb before the noun phrase, typical of poetic syntax.
segments.s690-g55: 'وزان' is the fused poetic preposition 'and/from that' (از + آن) meaning 'from that'. 'رزم' = 'battle'. 'جایی فتد' is an impersonal/ditransitive construction: 'a place fell' or 'a place befell' — idiomatically 'a place came to be' or 'there came to be a site'. 'دور دست' is the compound adjective 'distant, far‑off' (here split into 'دور' + 'دست' in the line). The clause says that as a result of that battle a place was located in a distant region.
segments.s690-g56: 'بسازد' is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive narrative form of 'ساختن' meaning 'he makes/builds' (in classical narrative often used for past events). 'بران' = 'upon that'/'thereon'. 'بوم' = 'dwelling/soil/homeland' (here 'dwelling/soil' where he builds). 'جای' = 'place', and 'نشست' = 'sat/settled' (past of 'to sit', used here as 'took up residence'). Taken together: 'he built on that soil a dwelling and settled there.'
segments.s690-g57: چو is the conditional 'if/when' introducing a subordinate clause. دوری گزیند is a verb phrase with دوری (avoidance/distance) as object and گزیند (گزیدن, 3sg present) 'chooses' as the verb; ز is the poetic preposition 'from', followed by فرمان تو 'your command' (فرمان = order, تو = your). The clause means 'if he chooses to be distant from your command.'
segments.s690-g58: بریزند is 3pl form (subjunctive/optative or narrative future) of ریختن 'to pour/shed' and here functions as 'they will spill' (blood). خونش is خون + enclitic -ش 'his blood' (direct object). به پیمان تو places the action 'for/by your covenant' (به = to/for/by, پیمان = covenant/pledge, تو = your). Together: 'they will spill his blood for your covenant.'
segments.s690-g59: بدو گفت: a standard narrative phrase 'to him said' (بدو = 'to him', گفت is the past of گفتن 'said' used here to introduce direct speech). خسرو is the speaker's name/title. جزین خود مباد is an optative formula: جز این 'except this' + خود 'itself/for yourself' + مباد (subjunctive negative particle) 'may there not be' — literally 'may there be nothing except this for you.'
segments.s690-g60: که here introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/which' referring back to 'this'. کردی is the perfect/past form 'you have done'. تو‌ای is the vocative/topic 'you — O'. پیرداننده یاد is a compound vocative: پیرداننده 'old-wise, preserver/keeper (of knowledge)' + یاد 'memory/remembrance' — an epithet addressed to the addressee. The clause clarifies what 'this' refers to: 'that which you have done, O preserver of memory.'
segments.s690-g61: چوگویی is a conditional phrase 'if you say / when you say' (here hypothetical 'if you say'). The following بدین (split here as ب + دین) functions as 'to/into this' (introducing what is being said). چند باشد درنگ: چند 'how much/how long', باشد is the subjunctive/polite present of بودن used here as 'would be/is', and درنگ 'delay/hesitation' — together 'how long would the delay be'.
segments.s690-g62: که introduces the result clause 'that/when'. آید is 3sg present/subjunctive of آمدن 'comes'. مرا is the oblique form 'me' (here marking possession/interest: 'to me'); را appears as the object/definiteness marker in the clause's word order. پادشاهی 'kingship/kingdom' is the thing that will come, and بچنگ (ب + چنگ) literally 'into the grip' meaning 'into my grasp'. The whole clause: 'that kingship will come into my grasp.'
segments.s690-g63: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed formula 'thus he gave an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد past of دادن 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the content of the answer. ده با دو ماه is a temporal expression 'ten with two months' i.e. 'ten plus two months' (i.e. twelve months) — با functioning as 'plus/with' and ماه 'months'.
segments.s690-g64: برین برگذرد is a verbal phrase where برین (بر + این) means 'over/at this (time)' and برگذرد (برگذرد, 3sg present/subjunctive of گذشتن) 'will pass/shall pass' — here idiomatically 'will come to pass by that time'. بازیابی کلاه: بازیابی 'recovery/restoration' + کلاه 'hat/crown' (کلاه used metonymically for crown) — together 'the recovery of the crown'. The clause means 'the recovery of the crown will occur by then.'
segments.s690-g9: به نزدیک دیر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/near the monastery' (به + place). آمد is the past tense verb 'came'. آواز داد is a light‑verb collocation (‘gave a voice’) meaning 'called out' or 'shouted'; داد is the light verb taking the noun آواز as its complement. The whole segment thus reports his approach and that he raised a voice.
segments.s690-g10: که introduces reported content ('that/that saying'). کردار تو is the noun + pronoun order ('your conduct' where the possessive pronoun follows the noun). جز ... مباد is a classical optative formula: جز = 'except', and مباد is an optative/negative particle meaning roughly 'may there not be' — together here giving the wish 'may there be nothing except worship'.
segments.s690-g11: گر begins a conditional ('if'). از دیر دیرینه = 'from the ancient/old monastery' (از marks source, دیر = monastery, دیرینه = 'ancient/long‑standing'). آیی is the present/subjunctive form 'you come' used in conditional clauses. فرود is an adverbial/nominal 'descent' (= 'down'); together آیی فرود = 'you come down/descend.'
segments.s690-g12: This line contains two optative wishes. زنیکی دهش literally 'a giving of nearness' (زنیکی = nearness, دهش = gift/grant) and باد is the optative particle 'may it be'. برتو درود attaches to the wish as a second blessing: برتو = 'upon you', درود = 'greeting/blessing'. Poetic word order places the nouns before the optative باد.
segments.s690-g13: هم آنگاه forms a temporal adverbial ('at that same moment' / 'then'). راهب is the subject 'the monk'. چو introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'when'; آوا شنید is 'he heard the sound' with شنید in the simple past. Word order places the temporal phrase before the subject and the subordinate clause after it.
segments.s690-g14: فرود آمد is the compound 'came down' (فرود 'descent' + آمد 'came'). از دیر gives the source 'from the monastery'. و links the clauses, and او را بدید is 'and he saw him' with را marking the definite object and بدید the past tense 'saw'.
segments.s690-g15: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت = 'said' — poetic reporting). خسرو تویی is an inverted predication: خسرو (Khosrow) + تویی ('you are') = 'you are Khosrow.' بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' adding emphasis to the statement.
segments.s690-g16: زتخت introduces the source/position 'from the throne' (ز = از 'from' fused to تخت). پدرگشته is a descriptive compound meaning 'of the father / paternal' (here indicating 'of his father' or 'his father's'), so زتخت پدرگشته reads 'from his father's throne.' نا + شادمان is the negative prefix + adjective meaning 'unhappy' or 'not joyful'; placed after the source phrase it predicates the state resulting from that loss.
segments.s690-g41: "ز گفتار او ماند خسرو شگفت" is literally ordered 'from/at her words remained Khosrow astonished.' ز is the old/poetic preposition introducing cause ('because of' / 'at'); گفتار او is the noun phrase 'her speech/utterance' (object of ز); ماند is the past/simple verb 'remained' and خسرو is the subject (proper name) placed after the verb for poetic order; شگفت is a predicative adjective meaning 'astonished/filled with wonder' that links to ماند (i.e. 'remained astonished').
segments.s690-g42: چو introduces a temporal clause here ('when'); شرم آمدش is a light impersonal construction 'shame came to him' (آمدش = آمد + enclitic pronoun = 'came to him'); پوزش اندر گرفت is a compound in which گرفت (take/seize) is the main verb and پوزش ('apology' / 'pardon') is its object, with اندر marking 'in/into' or intensifying the verb ('took up an apology' / 'made an apology'). The whole clause explains what Khosrow did when modesty/shame seized him.
segments.s690-g43: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (indirect object) placed before the verb; گفت (here a verbal form 'said') with راهب as the agent 'the monk said to him'; که introduces the content of the speech (a complementizer 'that' / direct speech marker); پوزش مکن is a negative imperative 'do not apologize.'
segments.s690-g44: بپرس is an imperative 'ask!' addressing the addressee; از من uses از as the source/target marker 'of/from me' with من as the object; the second از marks topic/subject matter ('about/concerning'); بودنیها is a plural abstract noun 'the beings/things that are' (i.e. existent things); سخن is the noun 'speech/talk'—together بپرس از من از بودنیها سخن = 'ask me; speak of the things that are.'
segments.s690-g45: بدین آمدن is an archaic/poetic circumstantial phrase meaning 'in/with this coming' (بدین = 'with/thus' + آمدن 'coming'); شاد و گستاخ باش is an imperative clause 'be joyful and bold/forward' where باش is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'be.' The phrase commands an attitude on arrival/at that event.
segments.s690-g46: جهان را is the definite object/topic 'the world' (را marks the direct object or topicalized noun); یکی here means 'one' or 'a single'; بارور شاخ is a noun phrase 'fruitful/fertile branch' (بارور modifies شاخ); باش is again the imperative 'be'—so the line urges the addressee to 'be a single fruitful branch for the world.'
segments.s690-g47: که here introduces a wish or purpose ('that' / 'so that' / optative); یزدان is the divine agent 'God'; تو را (تو + را) marks the beneficiary 'to you'; بی‌نیازی is the abstract noun 'lack of need' or 'self-sufficiency'; دهد is a 3rd-person present/optative verb 'may (he) give' — classical Persian uses دهد to express a blessing/wish ('may God give you ...').
segments.s690-g48: بلند اخترت is a possessive phrase 'your high/lofty star' (بلند 'high/lofty' qualifying اخترت 'your star'); سرفرازی is the abstract noun 'honor/glory' as the object; دهد again expresses a hopeful wish 'may (it) give' — together 'may your lofty star grant you honor.' The verb is parallel to the previous دهد and functions as an optative/benediction.
segments.s690-g105: بدو گفت خسرو is a poetic inversion meaning “Khosrow said to him”: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + خسرو (Khosrow). که introduces the content clause (“that”). از ترسگار is a prepositional phrase “out of/from (از) a cause of fear (ترسگار)” — ترسگار is an older/poetic noun meaning something that causes fear.
segments.s690-g106: نیاید is a negative optative/subjunctive sense here — ‘ought not to come / should not happen’. سخن گفت is the verbal construction ‘to speak’ (سخن + گفت(ن)), with گفت functioning as the verbal element; نابکار qualifies the manner/agent (‘wicked’/‘evil’). The whole phrase means roughly “wicked speech should not come / one should not speak wickedly.”
segments.s690-g107: ز تو نیز هرگز ندیدم بدی is literally ‘from you also never I saw evil’ — poetic word order places the verb ندیدم (I did not see) before or amid its arguments. ز تو (‘from you’) here means ‘in you’/‘about you’; نیز = ‘also’; هرگز = ‘never’; بدی = ‘evil’ (object).
segments.s690-g108: نیازی به is a noun phrase ‘a need for’ (نیازی here = ‘need’ rather than ‘to pray’), followed by the objects کژی و نابخردی ‘crookedness and folly’. In context the clause denies any need: ‘(there is) no need for crookedness and folly.’
segments.s690-g109: ولیکن = ‘but/yet’. ز کار سپهر بلند is a genitive/attributive phrase: ز کار = ‘on account of / because of the affair/work’; سپهر بلند = ‘the high/lofty sky/heavens’ (poetic order, adjective after noun is common). Together: ‘because of the lofty workings/affairs of the heavens.’
segments.s690-g110: نباشد شگفت is an idiom: ‘there would be no wonder / it would not be surprising’. ار introduces a conditional ‘if’. شوی is 2nd-person singular subjunctive ‘you become / you should become’. پر گزند = ‘full of harm’ (پر = ‘full (of)’, گزند = ‘harm’). The clause reads: ‘it would be no wonder if you became full of harm.’
segments.s690-g111: چو = ‘when’. بایسته کاری = ‘a fitting/necessary task’ (بایسته = necessary/fitting placed before the noun کاری). بود is the past-tense copula ‘was’. ایزدی postmodifies کاری: ‘a divine task’ (ایزدی = divine). The whole clause: ‘when a divine/ godly task was fitting/required.’
segments.s690-g112: بیکسو شود = ‘turns to one side / goes aside’ (بیکسو = ‘to one side/aside’; شود = ‘becomes / happens’). دانش و بخردی = ‘knowledge and wisdom’. The verb شود precedes the coordinated subject (typical poetic order); the sense is that knowledge and wisdom turn aside or are directed to one side in that circumstance.
segments.s690-g25: This is a temporal clause + main action: 'پرستنده' (the worshipper) is the subject, 'چون دید' is a subordinating temporal phrase 'when he saw', and 'بردش' is a past-tense verb with the enclitic object '-ش' ('him') meaning 'took/led him'. 'نماز' (prayer) appears after the verb as the object/goal of the taking. The word order (subject — temporal clause — verb + object enclitic — object noun) reflects poetic inversion common in classical Persian.
segments.s690-g26: 'سخن گفت' is a verb phrase meaning 'spoke' or 'held conversation'; here the verb 'گفت' is past tense. 'با او' is the prepositional phrase 'with him'. 'زمانی دراز' is an adverbial phrase 'for a long time' where 'زمانی' ('a span of time' / 'for a while') is qualified by the adjective 'دراز' ('long') — the adjective follows the noun, as is normal in Persian, forming the adverbial expression.
segments.s690-g27: This line uses the object-marker 'را' to mark 'آزمون' as the definite/direct object. 'یکی' (= 'one' or 'a certain') modifies 'آزمون'. 'بدو' is the clitic prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him'. The verb 'گفت' (said) follows these elements and 'شاه' (the king) appears after the verb — an OSV/order inversion typical of poetic Persian where the subject can come after the verb.
segments.s690-g28: The conjunction 'که' introduces reported speech ('that ...'). 'من' is the first-person subject. 'کهنری‌ام' (here spelled 'کهتری‌ام') is an adjective + first-person copular suffix '-ام' meaning 'I am lesser/inferior'. The preposition 'ز' (poetic for 'than/from') introduces the comparison 'ز ایران سپاه' = 'than the army of Iran', where 'ایران' qualifies 'سپاه' (army of Iran).
segments.s690-g29: 'پیامی' ('a message') is fronted as the object (indefinite by the suffix '-ی'). 'همی' is the poetic/verbal particle here that marks the verbal form (in classical Persian it often signals present habitual or a future-oriented sense). 'نزد قیصر' is a prepositional phrase 'to Caesar / in the presence of Caesar'. 'برم' is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form used in narrative to express 'I will take/carry'.
segments.s690-g30: 'چو' introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when'. 'پاسخ دهد' is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive construction 'he gives an answer' (classical narrative uses this to indicate a future/expected action). The main-clause verb 'برم' repeats the sense 'I will take/carry' and here its object (the message) is understood from the previous clause; 'سوی مهتر' = 'toward the chief' is the destination phrase.
segments.s690-g31: 'گرین رفتن' is a noun phrase built from an adjective 'گرین' ('tearful') + the infinitive/masdar 'رفتن' meaning 'departure/going', so 'my tearful going'. 'من' marks the possessor 'my'. 'همایون بود' is the predicate 'was auspicious' with 'بود' as the past copula. The whole clause states that the speaker's tearful departure was (surprisingly) auspicious.
segments.s690-g32: 'نگه کن' is an imperative 'look!' or 'behold!'. 'که' introduces a content clause meaning 'that / so that / to see that'. 'فرجام من چون بود' is an indirect interrogative/statement 'how/my end was' where 'چون' functions as 'how/what' and 'بود' is the past copula — literally 'see how my end was.' The noun 'فرجام' (end/outcome) precedes the possessive 'من'.
segments.s690-g1: ببود is an archaic past form meaning 'was' and introduces the state of the subject. اندر آن شهر خسرو is a normal prepositional phrase ('in that city Khosrow'); Persian allows the order Demonstrative/NP after the preposition. روز ('day') follows خسرو to mark the duration ('three days').
segments.s690-g2: چو here means 'when' (a temporal conjunction). بفروخت is a past form 'sold' (3rd person). گیتی فروز functions as a descriptive epithet (literally 'world‑bright' or 'world‑illuminating') — likely a title or epithet that follows the verb in this compressed poetic clause.
segments.s690-g3: بابر is the personal name/agent. اندر آورد is a light verb phrase 'brought in' (اندر + آورد). برنده تیغ is a noun phrase with the adjective/noun برنده 'cutting/sharp' modifying تیغ 'sword' (object of آورد).
segments.s690-g4: جهانجوی شد is a nominal+copula construction: جهانجوی ('world‑seeking', here 'an ambitious person') + شد 'became'. سوی راه وریغ is a directional/adverbial phrase: سوی 'toward', راه 'path/road', and وریغ 'wickedness/filth' — together 'toward the path of wickedness'.
segments.s690-g5: که introduces an explanatory or relative clause ('for' / 'that'). اوریغ بد نام is a sequence of adjectives before the noun: اوریغ (variant of وریغ, 'foul') + بد نام ('badly named' = infamous). آن شارستان is 'that city/town'. The order is poetic: adjectives then demonstrative then noun.
segments.s690-g6: بدو is a dative 'to it' or 'for it' used idiomatically to mark location/possession. در here means 'in/inside', followed by two nouns joined by و: چلیپا 'cross' and بیمارستان 'hospital' — literally 'to it (there was) in (it) a cross and a hospital.' This inverted order (dative + در + items) is typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s690-g7: ببی راه پیدا appears as a compound or name modifier placed before the noun: Bibi + راه پیدا ('path‑finding' / part of the name). یکی دیر بود is an indefinite existence statement: یکی 'a certain' + دیر 'monastery' + بود 'was' (past). The whole means 'there was a monastery called Bibi Rah‑peyda.'
segments.s690-g8: جهانجوی is the subject ('the world‑seeker' / ambitious man). آواز راهب is a genitive construction 'the voice of the monk' (N + N). شنود is an older narrative verb form meaning 'he hears' (often used for past action in classical Persian narrative), so here 'he heard.'
segments.s690-g81: بدو گفت راهب is a classical word order where the indirect object 'بدو' (to him) precedes the verb 'گفت' (said), followed by the subject 'راهب' (the monk). The particle 'که' introduces reported speech, and 'آری همین' is an emphatic interjection inside the quotation meaning “yes/indeed, exactly.”
segments.s690-g82: This clause is the content of the monk's speech. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'گستهم' is a noun/gerund meaning the speaker's wandering or roaming (literally “my wandering”); 'بینی' is 2nd-person singular present/future 'you see'; 'بسی' is an adverb meaning 'much/very' (poetic) modifying 'رنج و کین' (suffering and enmity).
segments.s690-g83: 'بدو گفت خسرو' again puts the dative 'بدو' before the verb 'گفت' with the speaker 'خسرو' following; 'که' introduces the speech. 'ای' is a vocative/exclamatory particle attached here to address or call attention ('O' or 'what!'), and 'رای زن' means 'the woman's opinion / counsel' (رای = counsel/opinion, زن = woman).
segments.s690-g84: 'ازان پس' = 'from that time on' (از + آن + پس); 'چه گویی' is a second-person present/subjunctive/question 'what do you say / what will you say' (here interrogative); 'چه خواهد بدن' uses the impersonal/third-person modal 'خواهد' (will/desire) with 'بدن' (body) as subject — 'what will the body want/desire?' The two 'چه' frame two related questions.
segments.s690-g85: 'بدو گفت راهب' as before marks 'the monk said to him'. Here 'مندیش' is an imperative/negative admonition in classical Persian meaning 'do not think / do not worry' (a prohibitive instruction), and 'زین' in the clause functions to point to 'this' or 'of this' (poetic/elliptical pointing to the preceding matter).
segments.s690-g86: 'کزان' = 'from which / from that' introduces the consequence; 'پس' = 'then/after'; 'نبینی' is 2nd-person singular negative form 'you will not see' (ن + بینی); 'جز' = 'except'; 'از آفرین' = 'but praise/ acclamation' (آفرین here = praise, applause). The clause states that henceforth you will see nothing except praise.
segments.s690-g87: 'نیاید' is a negative present/future (optative) meaning 'will not come / will not befall'; it combines with 'بروی' (second-person singular present/subjunctive of رفتن here used idiomatically as 'befall' or 'come upon') and the pronoun 'تو' (you). 'دیگر بدی' is the object phrase 'another/other evil/harm.' Together: 'no other harm will befall you.'
segments.s690-g88: 'مگر' here means 'except / unless'; it introduces the single qualification. 'سخت کاری' is a noun phrase 'a severe / hard deed or act' (سخت = severe), with 'کاری' functioning as 'act/work' (poetic use). 'بود ایزدی' uses the past/copular verb 'بود' in a modalizing sense ('would be / is') with 'ایزدی' qualifying it as 'divine' — 'unless it be a severe divine act.'
segments.s690-g73: بپرهیز is an imperative (avoid/beware). It is followed by the prepositional phrase زان مرد ناسودمند where زان = از + آن (‘from that’/‘of that’) and مرد ناسودمند is the noun phrase ‘that unprofitable/useless man.’ The verb comes before its object here (verb-first imperative common in classical Persian).
segments.s690-g74: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/for that'). باشدت is an optative/jussive form (classical usage) meaning roughly 'may there be to you' or 'may happen to you.' ز + (و) represents از او ('from him') and is the source marker; the sequence درد و رنج و گزند lists three coordinated nouns ('pain, suffering, and harm'). The whole clause explains the reason to avoid the man.
segments.s690-g75: بر آشفت is a verbal phrase meaning 'became angry/was disturbed' (بر as an inseparable element with آشفت). خسرو is the subject and appears immediately after the verbal phrase (inverted order typical in poetry). به بستام گفت is a prepositional phrase 'said to Bastam' (به + person + گفت).
segments.s690-g76: که introduces reported speech or content ('that/asking that'); با من سخن برگشا is an imperative-like request where برگشا is the imperative 'open/reveal' applied to سخن ('speech/talk'), and با من marks the addressee ('to me' / 'with me' — here 'speak to me'). از نهفت (lit. 'of the hidden') specifies the matter to be revealed. Word order places the imperative before its object in the poetic register.
segments.s690-g77: تو را is the fronted patient/topic (تو + object marker را) marking 'you' as the thing affected; مادرت is 'your mother' with the possessive suffix -ت. نام گستهم کرد is a verbal clause 'made the name Gostaham' = 'named (you) Gostaham' where کرد is the verb 'did/made.' Note the possessive suffix and the non‑canonical order (object before subject) used for emphasis.
segments.s690-g78: تو گویی که is an idiomatic poetic phrase meaning 'you seem/it is as if' (گویی = 'it seems / as if'). بستامم is the proper name Bastam with the 1st-person possessive suffix -م ('my Bastam'), and اندر نبرد is a locative phrase 'in battle.' The clause as a whole presents a simile: 'you seem as if (you are) my Bastam in battle.'
segments.s690-g79: به راهب چنین گفت is a reporting formula: به راهب ('to the monk') + چنین گفت ('said thus'). کینست is a nominal sentence meaning 'it is kīn' — here kīn = 'hatred/revenge' or 'vengeful feeling' — and خال is the noun 'mole/mark/spot' being predicated by کینست. Word order places the predicate-like element before or adjacent to the noun in poetic diction.
segments.s690-g80: به خون بود is 'it was with blood' or 'it was of blood' (بود as past copula), با مادر من همال says 'a companion/associate of my mother' (همال here 'fellow/companion' or 'bearer' figuratively). The prepositional phrases specify that the mark was associated in blood with his mother — a compact, elliptical classical phrasing.
segments.s690-g97: به here functions as the adverbial preposition 'by' (an oath/invocation) and heads a list of items sworn by: the proper name آذرگشسپ, then خورشید 'sun' and ماه 'moon' joined by و 'and'. The phrase is a string of ablative/adjunct items introduced by repeated به, a common poetic device for emphasis.
segments.s690-g98: Another به continues the series of oaths: 'by جان (life) and سر (head)'. نامبردار qualifies شاه and here means 'renowned' or 'well‑named'; the verb is understood/omitted, so this clause functions as an emphatic nominal phrase: 'by X and Y — (name) the renowned king.'
segments.s690-g99: به گفتار ترسا frames the source ('in/by the speech of the pious'). نگَر is an imperative 'consider/regard' and نگروی (from گرویدن 'to adopt, to take up') here expresses the idea of 'taking up' or 'being carried away by' that speech; together the line warns against heeding or adopting the pious one's words.
segments.s690-g100: سخن گفتن is a verbal-nominal phrase 'speech/speaking' and ناسزا 'insult/abuse' is its object; نشنوی (from شنیدن) is the verb 'you do not hear' / 'you will not hear' (negation is built into the meter/context). The clause states that one will not hear insulting speech (or: do not listen to abusive words).
segments.s690-g101: مرا ایمنی ده is an imperative request 'give me security/protection' where مرا is the object marker + pronoun. ز گفتار اوی means 'from his words' (ز = from; اوی = his). The word order is typical: object (مرا) + noun (ایمنی) + verb (ده 'give') + ablative phrase.
segments.s690-g102: چو سوگند خوردم is an idiomatic perfective clause 'for I have sworn an oath' (سوگند خوردم = 'I took/swore an oath'); بهانه مجوی is an imperative negative 'do not seek an excuse' (مجوی = do not seek). The conjunctional relation is causal/explanatory: because I swore, seek no pretext.
segments.s690-g103: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. هرگز نسازم is a negated present/future-like form 'I shall never make/do' (نسازم from ساختن 'to make/do' with negative particle). بدی 'evil' is the object; درنهان is an adverbial 'in secret/hiddenly' (poetic form). The clause expresses an emphatic denial of any secret wrongdoing.
segments.s690-g104: براندیش is an imperative 'reflect/consider' (addressing the addressee). از کردگار جهان supplies the object of reflection: 'of/from the Creator (کردگار) of the world (جهان)' — از here means 'concerning/from' in the sense 'think of the world's Creator.'
segments.s690-g65: اگر is the conditional 'if'. بر سر آید is an idiomatic verbal phrase: بر (on/upon) + سر (head) + آید (here 'happen') meaning 'come upon' or 'pass (over) the head' — i.e. 'to occur/pass'. ده وپنج روز is a simple numeral phrase 'ten and five days' = 'fifteen days' and functions as the temporal subject of آید.
segments.s690-g66: تو is the second‑person pronoun (poetic 'thou/you'). گردی here is an archaic/pedantic verbal form meaning 'become' (not the nominal senses listed), forming the predicate 'you shall become'. شهنشاه گیتی فروز is a nominal predicate: شهنشاه 'emperor' with the postpositive compound modifier گیتی فروز 'world‑illuminating/brightening the world' (فروز as descriptive element).
segments.s690-g67: بپرسید is the past narrative verb 'asked' (3rd sg. narrative). خسرو is the proper name (Khosrow) in apposition to the verb. کزین 'کز' + 'این' = 'from this/of this' marks the source (from this assembly), and انجمن is the noun 'assembly' (the group being addressed).
segments.s690-g68: که introduces a content/relative clause 'who/that'. کوشد is the present/iterative meaning 'strives/endeavors' or 'exerts himself' (here rendered 'endures/works'), followed by two prepositional complements begun by به 'in/with': به رنج 'in/with suffering' and به آزار تن 'in/with bodily torment' (تن = body). The clause asks for identification of the one who undergoes such hardship.
segments.s690-g69: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed narrative formula 'thus gave answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = past 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که begins the reported content clause. بستام here functions as a proper name in the reply (though the lemma list shows verbal senses), and نام is the noun 'name' that indicates what follows (i.e. 'let his name be ...').
segments.s690-g70: گوی is an archaic/formulaic verb form meaning 'call' or 'let him be called' (from گفتن), used here to proclaim a name. برمنش is an adjective 'of noble disposition' (بر‑ as an intensifier/prefix). باشد is the optative/jussive 'may he be' or present subjunctive used to express a wish. و coordinates the following quality شادکام 'fortunate/joyful'.
segments.s690-g71: دگر functions as 'another' / 'the other'. آنک is a relative 'the one who/that' introducing the description. خوانی is the present (2nd/3rd) form of خواندن 'to sing/read/call' here describing that person as 'the singer' or 'the one who sings'. و joins the next phrase; را is the definite object marker marking 'خال خویش' (his own beauty‑mark) as the object that the singer shows or points to. The final ش is the possessive suffix 'his/her/its'.
segments.s690-g72: بدو is a dative/adverbial 'to him' or 'unto him'. تازه is an adverb 'anew' / 'freshly'. دانی (from دانستن) here functions in a hortative/reporting sense 'you tell/you make known' (second person), so the clause means 'you newly make known to him...' مه و سال خویش 'his moon and year' = 'his month and year' (مه here = moon/month), with و coordinating the two temporal nouns and خوی + ش forming the possessive 'his own'.
segments.s690-g89: بر + آشوبد is a poetic verb phrase: بر is a prepositional particle 'upon/against' used here with the verb آشوبد (a classical/potential form of آشفتن/آشوبیدن) meaning 'become disturbed' or 'be set in turmoil'. In classical Persian the finite verb often precedes its subject, so آشوبد (3rd sg. subjunctive/optative sense 'may/become disturbed') comes before the subject این سرکش; آرام تو (literally 'your calm/peace') is the affected noun phrase (your peace). The word order and particles mark a wish/prospective: 'May this rebel disturb your peace.'
segments.s690-g90: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time on / after that'. نباشد is the negative form of باشد used impersonally here — 'there will not be / there will remain none' — followed by به جز 'except'. کام تو is the exception 'your desire/wish'. Together: 'From then on there will be nothing except your desire.'
segments.s690-g91: اگر چند is a classical concessive 'even if / although' (چند used adverbially here). بد گردد is a verbal phrase: بد is the predicate 'bad/unfavourable' and گردد (گردد = گشت‌/شد) is the 3rd singular present/subjunctive 'become' — 'if this suspicion becomes bad'. The subject این بدگمان follows the verb in the typical poetic order.
segments.s690-g92: همانش ('that very thing' = همان + -ش enclitic) is the resumed demonstrative; بدست تو is a locative/agent phrase 'into / in your hand' meaning 'under your control' (بدست = 'in the hand / into the hand'); باشد is the 3rd sg. subjunctive/potential 'will be' and زمان placed after the verb gives a temporal reading 'at (the right) time' or 'in time'. Word order here is typical poetic inversion: 'That thing will be in your hand at the time.'
segments.s690-g93: بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him / unto him' (بـ + او). گفت is the verb 'said' (perfect/past), and گستهم here is the proper name 'Gustaham' (a character) — in classical Persian the verb can precede the speaker's name. کای is the vocative particle 'O' and شهریار is the addressee 'king' (vocative). So: 'Gustaham said to him: "O king..."'.
segments.s690-g94: دلت را is 'your heart' as definite object (دل + enclitic -ت, plus object marker را). بدین here functions as 'about this / because of this' (بـ + این contracted), and هیچ is an intensifier 'at all / nothing' (negation/emphasis). رنجه مدار is an idiomatic prohibition: رنجه (root meaning 'be distressed; suffer') with مدار forming the prohibitive construction 'do not cause / do not suffer' — hence 'do not trouble/afflict your heart.' The whole clause is a negative imperative addressed to the king: 'Do not trouble your heart about this at all.'
segments.s690-g95: به here is an oath/introductory preposition 'by' (classical usage), پاکیزه is an adjective 'pure/clean', and یزدان is 'God / the divine'. که introduces a relative clause 'who' (که ماه آفرید = 'who created the moon'); آفرید is a past tense verb 'created'. The clause is an oath-like affirmation invoking the pure God who made the moon.
segments.s690-g96: جهان را is the direct object 'the world' (را marks definiteness/object). بسان تو is a simile 'like you' (بسان = 'like/as' + تو). شاه آفرید is the verb phrase 'created/made a king' with آفرید in past tense; the verb follows the object and simile phrase in poetic word order: 'He created the world, and made a king like you.'
segments.s690-g33: بدو گفت راهب is a V‑S phrase typical of classical Persian: 'to him said the monk' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said', راهب = 'the monk'). که introduces the reported or subordinate clause. چونین مگوی is an admonitory clause: چونین = 'thus/like this' and مگوی is the negative imperative form of مگو ('do not say'), here telling the addressee not to speak in that manner.
segments.s690-g34: This clause contains two linked prohibitions. توشاهی مکن is a contracted ordering of تو شاهی مکن ('do not be/act as a king'): توشاهی functions as 'as a king' and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. خویشتن is the emphatic/reflexive 'for yourself' or 'yourself', and شاه جوی is a compound nominal idea 'king‑seeking' / 'seeking kingship' (شاه + جوی 'search'), so together 'do not seek kingship for yourself.'
segments.s690-g35: چو دیدمت = 'when I saw you' (چو = 'when', دیدمت = past 'I saw you' with enclitic object -ت). گفتم is the past-tense verb 'I said/told', and سراسر سخن is the object phrase 'the entire speech/whole matter' placed after the verb in typical poetic order: 'I told the whole account.'
segments.s690-g36: مرا هر زمان آزمایش مکن is an imperative prohibition where مرا (here split orthographically into م + را) is the object 'me' marked by the postpositive object marker را, هر زمان = 'at every time / at every turn', and آزمایش مکن is the negative imperative 'do not test / do not try'. Word order places the object before the temporal adverb for emphasis.
segments.s690-g37: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not/should not be'; it governs the clause that follows. دروغ ایچ = 'falsehood at all' (ایچ here an emphatic negator 'at all'), and دردین تو = 'in your religion/faith' (در + دین + تو). The whole means 'there must not be any falsehood at all in your faith.'
segments.s690-g38: نه is a further negation 'nor/no', کژی = 'crookedness/wrongness', and برین is an archaic concatenation of بر + ین (بر = 'on/upon', ین ≈ این = 'this'), so برین راه = 'on this path'. و links آیین تو = 'your custom/practice'. Together: 'nor let there be crookedness on this way and your practice.'
segments.s690-g39: بسی رنج دیدی is an idiomatic past construction meaning 'you experienced/underwent much suffering' (بسی = 'much/many', رنج = 'suffering', دیدی = past 'you saw/experienced'). و آویختی adds 'and you clung/attached yourself' (آویختی = past of آویختن, here 'to cling to / hold fast'), preparing the contrast in the following clause.
segments.s690-g40: سرانجام = 'at last / finally', زین is an archaic contracted form equivalent to 'از این' ('from this') before the noun بنده 'servant'. بگریختی is the past 'you fled / ran away from' (ب + گریختی). The clause therefore says 'in the end you fled from this servant.'
segments.s690-g17: زدست is a prepositional phrase meaning “by the hand of / from the hand of.” یکی is an indefinite numeral here (“a certain / a single”), placed before the noun it modifies. بدکنش is a compound noun ‘bad‑doer’ (bad + action) and بنده‌ای is the indefinite noun “a servant” (‑ای marks indefiniteness or ‘one’). Word order is poetic: prepositional phrase + determiner + noun.
segments.s690-g18: پلیدی is the noun ‘vileness / filth’ serving as predicate; منی here reads as the speaker’s ‘my / I’ (classical genitive/possessive shape), followed by the copula ی (is). فش is a noun meaning ‘oppression / burden’ and پرستنده‌ای is an indefinite ‘a worshipper / devotee.’ The clause thus states an identity/attribution: “my vileness is (constitutes) a burden to the worshipper.”
segments.s690-g19: چوگفتار is a fused expression: چو as the temporal conjunction ‘when / as’ attached to گفتار ‘speech’ (i.e. ‘when he spoke’). راهب is the subject ‘the monk,’ and بی‌اندازه شد is the verbal predicate ‘became excessive / immeasurable’ (شد = became). The clause is a temporal subordinate: “when the monk’s speech became excessive.”
segments.s690-g20: دل خسرو is the possessive noun phrase ‘Khosrow’s heart.’ از here marks cause or source (‘because of / from’) and links to مهر او ‘his love/affection.’ تازه شد is a verbal phrase ‘became fresh / was renewed’ (شد = became). Overall: “Khosrow’s heart, because of his love, became renewed.”
segments.s690-g21: ز introduces cause/agent here (‘from / because of’), گفتار او is ‘his speech,’ در شگفتی is the locative phrase ‘in wonder/astonishment,’ and بماند (ب + ماند) is the narrative form ‘he remained / he stayed.’ The clause literally: “From his speech he remained in astonishment.”
segments.s690-g22: برو is an action verb ‘go’ (here narratively ‘he went’), بر + جهان‌آفرین = ‘to the World‑Creator’ (بر = toward/on to), and رابخواند is a past narrative verb meaning ‘he recited/read aloud’ (here: recited a prayer or invocation). The clause describes the subsequent action: he went and recited to the Creator.
segments.s690-g23: ز پشت صلیبی is a locative/causal phrase: ز = ‘from,’ پشت = ‘behind,’ صلیبی = ‘of the cross / cruciform’ (i.e. behind a crucifix). بیازید is the (poetic) verb ‘opened / spread’ (here used of a hand), and دست is the noun ‘hand.’ Word order puts the place phrase first, then the verb and the affected limb.
segments.s690-g24: بپرسیدن is the verbal infinitive/finite narrative form ‘to ask / asked’ used here to indicate purpose or the action taken; مرد is the subject ‘man,’ and یزدان‌پرست is an adjective‑noun ‘God‑worshipper / pious man.’ Together: ‘the pious man opened his hand to ask.’

sentences.s690-s57: Then the king said to the monk thus: "Be of a cheerful heart, and regarding your fortunes."
sentences.s690-s58: And from that monastery, like a dazzling lightning, an uproar came toward the town — and ill will.
sentences.s690-s59: The city's great men received him — a man who was from among the people, for...
sentences.s690-s25: From the Caesar you obtain arms and an army; a certain maiden from the palace of crown and court.
sentences.s690-s26: When among the servants the affair was a lament, the sovereign was alert and your ally.
sentences.s690-s27: At last that ill‑born one will flee; he will often recall his good days.
sentences.s690-s28: From that battle a place befell in a far land; he built a dwelling there and took his seat.
sentences.s690-s29: If he chooses to keep away from your command, they will spill his blood for your covenant.
sentences.s690-s30: Khosrow said to him, 'May there be nothing besides this for you — O preserver of memory.'
sentences.s690-s31: If you say that, how long will the delay be before kingship comes into my grasp?
sentences.s690-s32: He answered thus: that in ten plus two months the recovery of the crown will take place.
sentences.s690-s5: He came up near the monastery and called out, "May your conduct be nothing but worship."
sentences.s690-s6: If you come down from the old monastery, may nearness be granted — greetings be upon you.
sentences.s690-s7: At that moment the monk, when he heard the sound, came down from the monastery and saw him.
sentences.s690-s8: He said to him, 'You are Khosrow, without doubt — unhappy since (or because of) being removed from your father's throne.'
sentences.s690-s21: Khosrow stood amazed at her words; when shame came upon him, he offered (or took) an apology.
sentences.s690-s22: The monk said to him, 'Do not apologize; ask me — speak of the things that are.'
sentences.s690-s23: In coming thus, be joyful and bold; be for the world a single fruitful branch.
sentences.s690-s24: May God grant you freedom from need; may your high star grant you honor.
sentences.s690-s53: Khosrow said to him that one should not, out of fear, speak like a wicked man.
sentences.s690-s54: Nor have I ever seen any evil in you that required crookedness and folly.
sentences.s690-s55: But it would not be surprising — if you become full of harm — because of the lofty workings of the heavens.
sentences.s690-s56: When a divine task was required, knowledge and wisdom turned aside to one side.
sentences.s690-s13: When the worshipper saw him, he led him to prayer and spoke with him for a long time.
sentences.s690-s14: The king said to him one trial: that I am inferior to the army of Iran.
sentences.s690-s15: I will carry a message to Caesar; when he gives a reply, I will carry it to the chief.
sentences.s690-s16: My tearful departure was auspicious; look and see what my end was.
sentences.s690-s1: Khosrow was in that city for three days; on the fourth, when (the) world‑brightener sold...
sentences.s690-s2: Babr brought in a sharp sword; he became a world‑seeker, turned toward the road of wickedness.
sentences.s690-s3: For that town was foul and ill‑named; in it there was a cross and a hospital.
sentences.s690-s4: There was a monastery called Bibi Rah‑peyda; the world‑seeker heard the monk's voice.
sentences.s690-s41: The monk said to him, “Yes — indeed, from my wandering you will see much suffering and hatred.”
sentences.s690-s42: Khosrow said to him, “O — what is the woman's counsel? From now on what will you say, and what will the body desire?”
sentences.s690-s43: The monk said to him, “Do not worry about this; from that you will see nothing except praise.”
sentences.s690-s44: No other harm will befall you, unless it is a severe divine act.
sentences.s690-s37: Avoid that unprofitable man, for from him may come to you pain, suffering, and harm.
sentences.s690-s38: Khosrow, enraged, said to Bastam, 'Speak to me plainly about what is hidden.'
sentences.s690-s39: Your mother named you Gostaham; you seem as if you were my Bastam in battle.
sentences.s690-s40: He said to the monk thus: 'This spot/mole is of hatred; it was, in blood, a companion to my mother.'
sentences.s690-s49: By Azar‑Gashasp and by the sun and the moon, by life and by head, name the renowned king.
sentences.s690-s50: Do not be taken by the words of the pious; do not adopt (them) — you will not hear speech of insult.
sentences.s690-s51: Give me safety from his words; I have taken an oath — seek no pretext.
sentences.s690-s52: Know that I will never do evil in secret; reflect upon the Maker of the world.
sentences.s690-s33: If fifteen days come upon (your) head, you will become the world‑illuminating emperor.
sentences.s690-s34: Khosrow asked, of that assembly, who it was that endured hardship and bodily torment.
sentences.s690-s35: Thus was the reply given: 'Let his name be Bastam; may he be noble‑minded and fortunate.'
sentences.s690-s36: Another — the one who sings and displays his own beauty‑mark — you shall newly tell (or show) his month and year.
sentences.s690-s45: Let this unruly one disturb your peace; after that there will be nothing except your desire.
sentences.s690-s46: Even if this suspicion turns out ill, that very thing will be in your hand at the right time.
sentences.s690-s47: Gustaham said to him, 'O king, do not trouble your heart about this at all.'
sentences.s690-s48: By the pure God who created the moon — he created the world and made a king like you.
sentences.s690-s17: The monk said to him, "Do not speak thus; do not act like a king nor seek kingship for yourself."
sentences.s690-s18: When I saw you I told the whole matter; do not test me at every turn.
sentences.s690-s19: There should be no falsehood at all in your religion; nor any crookedness in this path and your practice.
sentences.s690-s20: You endured much suffering and clung (to me); in the end you fled from this servant.
sentences.s690-s9: By the hand of a single wicked servant; my vileness is a burden on the worshipper.
sentences.s690-s10: When the monk's speech grew excessive, Khosrow's heart was freshly stirred by his love.
sentences.s690-s11: He remained in astonishment at his words, and went to the World‑Creator and recited (prayer).
sentences.s690-s12: From behind the crucifix he opened his hand to ask — the God‑worshipping man.
